summary: A summary of two lectures given by Zhenan Bao at Cornell University detailing her research on skin-inspired electronics and dynamic polymers. She discusses overcoming charge transport issues in polymer materials to create stretchable circuits, and using nature-inspired dynamic bonds to engineer self-healing, shape-changing soft robotics and bioelectronics.

principles.statement: Look to nature's use of weak molecular interactions to design highly organized, functional structures.
principles.rationale: In nature, weak interactions between molecules lead to highly organized structures that perform specific jobs. Applying this principle to polymer chains via periodically placed non-covalent dynamic bonds yields tunable, self-healing, and stretchable materials.

mental_models.name: Skin-inspired engineering
mental_models.description: Using the properties of human skin—such as stretchability, self-healing, and multi-modal sensing—as a blueprint for developing new electronic materials and neuromorphic systems.
mental_models.example: Developing nano-confined polymer semiconductors that mimic skin-like functions while maintaining efficient charge transport, enabling soft sensors that measure pressure, movement, and bodily signals.

mental_models.name: Biomimetic dynamic bonding
mental_models.description: Emulating nature's use of weak, dynamic molecular interactions to create materials that can adapt, self-heal, or change shape on demand.
mental_models.example: Creating flexible polymer chains with dynamic bonds placed at regular intervals and pointing in specific directions to develop 'morphing' electronics and reconfigurable soft robots.
